speaker: interviewer
text: This interview is with Esther Rudomin Hautzig.

speaker: interviewee
text: The dog tag I just mentioned before was found about three or four weeks after my father's-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I guess you would call it-- division went actually to the front lines. And maybe it wasn't four weeks, maybe it was closer to three weeks. I never thought about the time, but of course, we must know it, chronologically. This was in the beginning, say, in the middle of September. In the middle of October, my father came to Vilna as if he would really had risen from the dead. And the first stop he made was to my grandmother's, on my mother's side. Her name was Sonia Zunser. She was my mother's second mother. My mother's birth mother died when my mother was three, and she was brought up by Sonia Zunser, who was a saint. And we'll talk about her later, I hope. But my father went to her house first. And there was my Uncle Liusik and Margola. And apparently the first thing my grandmother Sonia said, 'I didn't make your favorite chicken.' And the story was that my father and all the soldiers in his unit were given permission to discard their dog-- their dog tags, is what they were called, and uniforms, and to make their way wherever they could, whichever way they could. The colonel gave them leave. And he took off his uniform and remained in his-- and he had to take off his boots-- and he remained in his underwear, and he picked up a blanket, and a beret, and a safety pin, and a pair of shoes in bags that were left by people who were running east from the Germans. They were carrying things on their backs, you see, and they dropped them as the loads got too heavy. And he pretended that he was crazy. And he walked and walked and walked, for days and days and days. He walked nights, and he hid during the days in the woods. And at one point, he fell asleep. And a tank-- a German tank-- was just a few feet away from him. But it moved, and he was saved. You see, you never know how people are saved. A few feet of space between a man's head and a tank, and the man is dead. Eventually, he made his way to a town called Czestochowa, which is spelled, I guess, in the English, Czestochowa, C-H-E-S-T-O-C-H-O-W-A. Czestochowa, where he had business associates from before the war. Along the way, he stopped at the-- he had some money, which he took out of his uniform, and put it in his underwear.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he had some money, and he bought pants that were too short and a greasy shirt from this farmer who let him sleep in-- in the barn as he was running. Running-- he was escaping. He was going into the German territory, rather than east. He was going west because he felt that-- I don't know why.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he knew people in a town which was fairly close within actual walking, and walking, and walking distance. And he came to these people's house, and introduced himself. And they didn't believe him, because he didn't look like Mr. Samuel Rudominski, from Vilna, [INAUDIBLE]. And he reminded them of things that they bought, and they did together. And then they hid him. The Germans were already there. And they gave space in a little room behind the kitchen and shared their rations with him. And then when the peace pact was signed-- oh, that's why. It was still war. He went to the police precinct, changed his name from Samuel, which was Jewish, to Stanislav Rudominski. Said he had no papers, and they gave him traveling documents. And he made his way from Czestochowa to Warsaw. And in Warsaw, my mother had family, and he stopped in there. And then, he made his way all the way east to Bialystok, and from Bialystok by train to Vilna. And that's how he survived in '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a little girl, did you believe he was alive as your mother thought or dead as everybody else thought?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I believed anything my mother would tell me. If he said-- if she said he was alive, he was alive. You know? And actually, Margola came to live with us-- my mother's sister. And she came to school to get me. So Margola wasn't home when my father went to his mother-in-law's house. Liusik and Baba Sonia-- my grandmother Sonia-- Margola stayed with us because people were afraid. And what's worst is, three days before my father came, it was on October 16th, 1939, two days before my birthday and their anniversary. I was born my parent's second anniversary. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: He came, and Margola came to school to fetch me. And she was so afraid that there'll be such a shock, you know? She was so gentle. She went to the principal's office. I even know the principal's name. Her name was-- now it escapes me, now that I think of it. Because I'm so thinking about the story, remembering how she came to the principal's office.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was called into the principal's office, and she told me that, you know, I have some news for you, but don't be shocked. And she said, you know, Papa-- your Papa is here, Tata-- Papa. And I said I always knew he would be. They talked about it until the war-- real war started in '41. [NON-ENGLISH]. I always knew he would come. No wonder they thought my mother was nuts, and was taking me along for the ride. And he was there, and we had an anniversary and birthday celebration on the 18th. And there was a pretty good period for about a year, year and a half, when Russia gave Vilna to Lithuania, and we became the capital of the Lithuanian coun-- of Lithuania. And business was terrific.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was a birthday celebrated then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh my god. Through the '30s, I think I was the only person in-- that I knew in Vilna who had home movies rented. My father would get a movie projector, and Charlie Chaplin movies. The whole family gathered. We had a dining room which had French doors into my father's study.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the-- the screen was put up in the study, and the projector-- very big, sort of cumbersome thing-- was put in-- in the dining room. And there was Charlie Chaplin silent movies. But on the 18th of October 1939, there was just such commotion that papa came, we didn't need a movie, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And a few weeks later, I went to see with Shirley Temple, which is a movie about a father who was lost in the war. It's based on a story by Frances Hodgson Burnett. And he cried, and I cried. Oh, I'll never forget it. And I still watch on television and remember going in Vilna with my father who came from the dead, just like the father in the movie. And then, of course, second World War was becoming more and more of a reality in Vilna. Even though we didn't have a German occupation, we had a lot of people from Warsaw and Lodz and central Poland who were running east. And everyone took in refugees into their homes. And we had people coming and going, sleeping on the sofa in the dining room and in the study.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did non-Jewish people take in Jewish refugees?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I know of. I don't say they didn't, I just don't know. I'm very careful about making such statements because if I don't know, maybe they did. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And now we will come to-- to this enormous change in our lives, but I must say that having been sent to Siberia was a blessing in disguise for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did this happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1941, the three Baltic countries-- Lithuania, Latvia, and Estonia-- where annexed by the Soviet Union. Businesses were [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] taken away from their owners. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Soviets ruled the three Baltic countries. The Russian soldiers came in. Russian administrators came in. And in June, 1939-- it began earlier, actually, but I didn't know about it-- Soviet government soldiers came into homes of so-called enemies of the people-- capitalists and enemies of the people-- and arrested us with bayonets held over our heads. Everyone sat with a soldier standing over us. My father was called by an uncle to see what was happening in-- in their father's house across the landing. And he went there, and he didn't come back until the soldiers pushed him, in his pajamas, into our apartment. And we were arrested, put on trucks, sent on the trucks to the railroad station, and put into cattle cars. We didn't know where we were going.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your whole family kept together?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents and my grandparents. But at the station, my grandfather was separated from us, and put in a different echelon. That's what it's called. These trains were called echelons. And my grandmother shrieked and carried on. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was just so relieved that my father wasn't taken that I'm-- you know, I am not prettifying things. I tell it as I remember it from my child's point of view. I was so relieved that my father was with us. And we were put in this cattle car with about 30 or 35 other people, and we traveled for six weeks to Siberia. And as we were leaving our house on the truck, people were crying and lamenting and carrying on. Musik's mother and his sister were by the truck. My grandmother was there-- my mother's mother. But they had it so much worse, because they remained in Vilna. And the Germans came, and they were put in ghettos, And they were killed. And Musik's sister and mother were killed during the first few months of German occupation by being sent to Ponar, which was-- it's one of the five places [INAUDIBLE], which is a memorial killing woods. And we were in Siberia. And everybody lamented our going to Siberia, but it would-- would've been wonderful if everyone was sent to Siberia. My mother's brother came to the door-- Liusik-- to see what was going on. And the doorbell rang, and my mother was told by the soldier to open it. And she opened it and there was Liusik.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I remember where I sat. I could see it through the door. And the soldier asked my mother, 'Who is this?' And she said, 'I never saw that man in my life.' [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She tried to protect him. I think she spent the rest of her life blaming herself that she didn't say it's my brother. And then he would have been pulled in and taken away too. When you were taken from your home, how much time were you given, and what did you grab to take with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have a chance to grab anything except the photo album. I had photo albums by my bed, on a night table-- nightstand. Some people have books on their nightstands. I had family photo albums, because I used to look at them before I went to sleep. And I went-- I was-- we were all permitted to take some things. And I went and took my albums and came into the dining room, and my mother ordered me to put them back. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She was afraid that people-- the soldiers would see the other photographs. That was in her mind. And I've never-- I've never-- I don't know. I've never gotten over that. It's like losing your life when you have no record of your childhood, and of your mother as a young woman. Luckily, Miss Rachel-- Rochelle-- my governess had some pictures in Jerusalem after the war, and I have five or six pictures, which perhaps we will have a chance to look at. And so, after six weeks, we were deposited on the Siberian Steppe. S-T-E-P-P-E, which actually is a prairie. Flat land.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in the train for six weeks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were with which family members of these six weeks?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandmother and my parents in that car-- that echelon.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the conditions there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Compared to trains going to Treblinka or to Auschwitz, it was fine. But people were squeezed on these [INAUDIBLE]. They were like, you know, platform beds. We slept like sardines, but we didn't have to stand. Everything is relative.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a child, you had no idea what was going on anywhere else in the country, correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew the war was going on, but a war that isn't in your backyard is a very amorphous thing. It's just-- it's happening somewhere, but it doesn't really touch you. But once I got in that cattle car, I knew what war was, because that, for me, was war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they feed you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we got some watery soup, and bread. And when we stopped at various stops, people were coming to sell things, you know, peasants. And we had no windows. There were just small openings on the top [INAUDIBLE], on the top platform where people slept. And people who had money could buy sometimes a piece of cheese or bread or whatever. But there were peasants. There wasn't just rich people in our-- in our echelon car. There were peasants, and they brought food. And they actually offered it to those who didn't bring any food. But my mother would never accept any. Wouldn't let me, not that I would do it without her permission. So we got to Siberia after six weeks, and we were put on the marketplace [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like slaves. Just around the marketplace, like an open square where, on-- on Sundays people sold stuff in stands and barrels and whatever. And people were just placed around it with our bags or-- or suitcases. And people came around and looked at us, like-- you know, the only thing is I said to someone, they didn't look at our mouths, whether our teeth were all right. It was like a slave market. And my parents and my grandmother and I were sent to this alabaster mine-- gypsum mine, the stuff of which casts are made. And my mother was a dynamite chief of a group of about 10 women. And my father was a horse and buggy driver. And he could never master the-- the-- how do you say when the horse has to be put--

speaker: interviewer
text: Harnessed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Harnessed! Harness a horse. The horse would walk away, and he would sit there. I mean, we-- we laughed even then, because my father didn't consider it, look how terrible. He used to laugh his head off over this, you see? So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] if your parent laughs, you laugh too. But it was easy to laugh. It wasn't Ponar. We didn't know about Ponar.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the living conditions?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we were at that mine, the gypsum mine, the people who were-- it was June. No. It was middle of July, 1941. We were taken out of Vilna on June 7, 1941. And I can say that I remember the date. Just not too long ago I met someone who said-- do you know-- we spoke that day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he said, do you know it's, whatever, 50 years that we were sent to Siberia? And I said, how do you know? He said, don't you remember, it was June 7th? I said, I didn't even know it was June 7. So we got there in the middle of July. And we were 26 people in a classroom, and we slept on the floor, on-- whoever had blankets or straw mats. But it still was not like life in the ghetto. It was really rough and-- and difficult. But comparatively speaking, it was-- but we didn't have comparisons then. We didn't know. Were there bathrooms?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Outhouses. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Communal, an outhouse. And you washed at the river. There was a stream, and you washed your things at the stream, and sort of put them on the ground to dry. Very primitive.

speaker: interviewer
text: I love doing laundry now in the machine and the dryer. And the clean smell, and folding it all so nicely, you know? Some people ask me, has life in Siberia helped you cope with life troubles now? And I said, not with everything, but I sure like a bad, bad storm when I'm in New York, and I go home, and I take off my wet shoes, and I have tea and toast and jam. And I love doing laundry.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it's so minute. But life is made up of minute little pleasures. Every little thing that you enjoy makes life worthwhile.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you living in the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Through the summer. Then in September, 1941, or early October, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a pact was made by the Polish government in exile in London with the Soviet government in Moscow. We were Polish citizens, after all, even though we were in Lithuania. But our papers, our passports, whatever legal papers my parents had, were still under the Polish government. And so the people who were deported from former Poland to Siberia were gotten amnesty. We were no longer prisoners, or enemies of the state, in this alabaster mine. And we were permitted to live in the village of Rubtsovsk. And we moved there, and we got housing with another family. We were 14 people in one room, which included the kitchen-- everything. I mean, one room. And my family had a space like a platform with a mattress-- straw mattress. And another family-- the Segals, S-E-G-A-L-S, the Segals, from Vilna also, had the other bed. So four of us slept on each bed. And if one had to turn around at night, then you had to tap the people next to you so they could turn around with you. And there was a Russian couple and a baby. And there was a stove and a place to eat, and a little, like a anteroom, sort of like a shed, which was a-- these were all [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] mud houses, you know, made out of mud blocks. They were-- the fancy houses in [INAUDIBLE] were made of logs. They were log cabins. But the houses we lived in were really mud huts. The hut-- the first hut we lived in had a wooden floor. The others didn't. We had sort of mud on the floor, which was like white-washed. And this is where we lived, and my father worked in a [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what a [NON-ENGLISH] is, a communal farm. And he worked in their office because he was very good with figures. He was an electrical engineer, but he was an excellent sort of bookkeeper. And my mother was put to work in the bakery. She always drew the-- the hardest work. But she could eat in the bakery. So we were able to use her ration of bread. And I started school. I got to go to school there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's back up just a minute. How did you find a place to live?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to go to the-- what was virtually the police station, and we were assigned to families who would have to take us in. They weren't thrilled to have us, believe me. But all these people came from different-- from that gypsum mine and some-- it's not hundreds. You know, it was maybe 150, 120 people came. And we were placed-- when the government tells you you have to take somebody in, you just do. We had to take in what was called the village bum later on-- Vanya the bum-- when we had our own little hut. We were assigned to this family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you find work? Not you, but your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was assigned also, by-- everything was assigned. The communist government gave people no choice. Mula Rudomin went to work as a bookkeeper, and Raja Rudomin went to work in the bakery. My grandmother was too old to work any kind of a job, and I went to school. And I was assigned to a school, and that's where I went.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was this school from your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: From that first hut, it wasn't very far. It was maybe a 15 minute walk. Other schools-- another school I went to there, I had a very long walk through snowy storms and bad weather. But this was fairly close. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was just a [NON-ENGLISH] school. It wasn't a-- the elementary school or the-- for all I know it was the school for the whole-- for the whole village. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Michael-- Mikhail Gorbachev's wife, Lisa Gorbachev, grew up in Rubtsovsk. I'm sure we went to the same school. She's two years younger than I. And I didn't believe it when someone said that she grew up in Rubtsovsk. But in her autobiography, indeed it says that. So that was our first Fall. Autumn.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language are they speaking in that school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Russian, and I didn't-- I came to Russia without knowing Russian. I knew Polish and Yiddish, so I was illiterate. And it was very hard, but still, compared to what was happening to my cousins, it was minor. But to me, at that time, it wasn't. You know, the Sh-- the Holocaust has many faces, and everything is relative. Someone asked me in a school where I volunteered three years ago, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was told you're a Holocaust survivor. She was a substitute teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said well, I survived the war in Europe. She said, show me your number. And I said, I don't have a number. Well, then, how can you call yourself a Holocaust survivor? I said, I don't. Others do. I survived the war. And the war in Europe was the Holocaust for Jews. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Another guilt-provoking nail into my system. How can you call-- I didn't. I didn't have a number, and she did. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Where's your number? Like that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes one wishes that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't say wishes. I have-- I get a lot of letters from children, because I've written a book about our years in Siberia. And this book, S-T-E-P-P-E-- has a lot of readers. It's 27 years now that the book was published. It was published in 1968, when books about the war-- the Second World War were not that many. It's still in print and it's still read and studied and whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I got a letter about three or four years ago from a boy who said, aren't you glad that you were saved? God must have had a reason to save you. And I thought to myself, I wish I knew how to prove it to God that there was a purpose. It's hard, especially when someone says where's your number? So you begin to think, well, why am I here and Musik is not here? But that isn't just that day. It's every day. Survivors of all kinds live with all kinds of guilt.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said that at the meeting of the American Jewish Library Association. And a woman who survived a camp got up, very angry, and she said, I don't feel any guilt. I said, well, I do. I do. It's not a sense of guilt that I've done something wrong, but it's a sense of, why am I here and others are not. Like this little boy said, maybe God had a purpose.
